_meta.purpose: Curated SSOT for /pcos/ topic hub. Diagnostic-criteria comparison and phenotype frameworks rendered as Astro card grids.
_meta.source: Rotterdam 2003 consensus (Fertility & Sterility), AE-PCOS 2006 position, NIH 1990 working definition, 2023 International Evidence-Based PCOS Guideline (Teede et al. via Costello 2019 ANZJOG extract in the library), plus phenotype literature.
_meta.card_notes: Each card field is an HTML fragment with inline citation hyperlinks to library records. Render with Astro set:html. Inline citations are MANDATORY per editorial policy.

diagnostic_criteria.name: NIH 1990
diagnostic_criteria.subtitle: Zawadzki & Dunaif working definition
diagnostic_criteria.required_features_html: Both of: (1) chronic anovulation, (2) clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism. Other etiologies excluded.
diagnostic_criteria.prevalence_yield_html: Roughly 6-10% of reproductive-age women in population-based estimates.
diagnostic_criteria.stance_html: The narrowest definition. Captures the metabolically-loaded classic phenotype but excludes ovulatory hyperandrogenism and the normo-androgenic anovulation pattern.

diagnostic_criteria.name: Rotterdam 2003
diagnostic_criteria.subtitle: ESHRE / ASRM consensus
diagnostic_criteria.required_features_html: Two of three: (1) oligo- or anovulation, (2) clinical or biochemical hyperandrogenism, (3) polycystic ovaries on ultrasound. Other etiologies excluded.
diagnostic_criteria.polycystic_ovaries_required: Counts as a criterion (one of three)
diagnostic_criteria.prevalence_yield_html: Expands prevalence to roughly 10-15% by adding the ovulatory-hyperandrogenic and normo-androgenic-anovulation phenotypes (see Deswal 2021).
diagnostic_criteria.stance_html: The most widely used framework in clinical practice and the source of the four-phenotype classification. Anchored on the 2003 ESHRE/ASRM consensus paper.

diagnostic_criteria.name: AE-PCOS 2006
diagnostic_criteria.subtitle: Androgen Excess and PCOS Society
diagnostic_criteria.required_features_html: Hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical) is required, plus either anovulation or polycystic ovaries.
diagnostic_criteria.prevalence_yield_html: Yields a tighter androgen-centred cohort than Rotterdam and excludes the normo-androgenic Phenotype D.
diagnostic_criteria.stance_html: Frames PCOS as fundamentally a hyperandrogenic disorder. Useful when characterising metabolic and cardiovascular risk, which tracks more closely with androgen excess than with ultrasound morphology.

diagnostic_criteria.name: International 2023
diagnostic_criteria.subtitle: Evidence-Based Guideline (Teede et al.)
diagnostic_criteria.issuing_body: International consortium, endorsed by ESHRE, ASRM, and 39 societies
diagnostic_criteria.required_features_html: Retains Rotterdam two-of-three. Adds anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) as an acceptable substitute for ultrasound in adults; permits polycystic-ovarian morphology on transvaginal ultrasound only with high-frequency probes.
diagnostic_criteria.prevalence_yield_html: Broadly comparable to Rotterdam, with somewhat tighter ultrasound criteria and clearer cautions in adolescents.
diagnostic_criteria.stance_html: Current international consensus. Extends Rotterdam, addresses adolescent over-diagnosis explicitly, and standardises AMH use. Anchored in the library via Costello et al. 2019 ANZJOG, the international guideline's infertility-treatment extract.

phenotypes_rotterdam.name: Phenotype A
phenotypes_rotterdam.subtitle: Classic / full Rotterdam
phenotypes_rotterdam.features_html: Hyperandrogenism + ovulatory dysfunction + polycystic ovarian morphology.
phenotypes_rotterdam.metabolic_load_html: Highest metabolic load. Insulin resistance, central adiposity, dyslipidaemia, and hypertension cluster here. Bil et al. 2016 documents this pattern.

phenotypes_rotterdam.name: Phenotype B
phenotypes_rotterdam.subtitle: Hyperandrogenic anovulatory, normal ovaries on US
phenotypes_rotterdam.features_html: Hyperandrogenism + ovulatory dysfunction, normal ovarian morphology.
phenotypes_rotterdam.metabolic_load_html: Metabolic risk similar to Phenotype A. The absence of polycystic morphology on ultrasound does not protect against insulin resistance.

phenotypes_rotterdam.name: Phenotype C
phenotypes_rotterdam.subtitle: Ovulatory hyperandrogenic
phenotypes_rotterdam.features_html: Hyperandrogenism + polycystic ovarian morphology, but ovulation is preserved.
phenotypes_rotterdam.metabolic_load_html: Intermediate metabolic risk. Women in this group often present with cosmetic complaints (acne, hirsutism) and may be told they "don't really have PCOS" because cycles are regular.

phenotypes_rotterdam.name: Phenotype D
phenotypes_rotterdam.subtitle: Normo-androgenic anovulatory
phenotypes_rotterdam.features_html: Ovulatory dysfunction + polycystic ovarian morphology, no androgen excess.
phenotypes_rotterdam.metabolic_load_html: Lowest metabolic load of the four. Excluded by the AE-PCOS 2006 criteria. Bil et al. 2016 documents the lower metabolic burden of this phenotype relative to A and B.

phenotypes_rrm.name: Insulin-resistant
phenotypes_rrm.subtitle: Metabolic driver
phenotypes_rrm.features_html: Elevated fasting insulin or HOMA-IR, central adiposity, often acanthosis nigricans. The dominant pattern in most clinic populations.
phenotypes_rrm.intervention_html: Lifestyle modification anchored on dietary pattern and resistance training; insulin-sensitising agents such as myo-inositol and metformin, used per evidence and tolerability.

phenotypes_rrm.name: Inflammatory
phenotypes_rrm.subtitle: Chronic low-grade inflammation
phenotypes_rrm.features_html: Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ferritin), often with skin or gut symptoms, fatigue, joint complaints. May overlap with insulin-resistant pattern.
phenotypes_rrm.intervention_html: Identify and address upstream drivers (diet pattern, gut health per microbiome literature, sleep, environmental exposures) before defaulting to suppressive therapy.

phenotypes_rrm.name: Adrenal
phenotypes_rrm.subtitle: DHEA-S driven
phenotypes_rrm.features_html: Elevated DHEA-S with normal-to-mildly elevated testosterone, often a stress-physiology component, frequently lower BMI than the insulin-resistant pattern.
phenotypes_rrm.intervention_html: Workup must exclude non-classical congenital adrenal hyperplasia. Management focuses on stress-axis regulation rather than ovarian suppression.

phenotypes_rrm.name: Post-pill
phenotypes_rrm.subtitle: Hormonal-contraceptive unmasking
phenotypes_rrm.features_html: Symptoms emerge or worsen after discontinuing combined hormonal contraception. May represent unmasking of pre-existing PCOS or transient post-pill hormonal disruption.
phenotypes_rrm.intervention_html: Charting cycles to establish a real baseline; targeted workup before assuming "PCOS." Hormonal contraception does not treat PCOS, and combined contraceptives have documented unfavourable metabolic effects in this population per Mosorin et al. 2023.
